title: Calculating a z interval for a proportion
description: Practice constructing a one-sample z interval for a proportion.
topic_path: Calculating a z interval for a proportion

assessment_items.question_content:
Della has over $500$ songs on her mobile phone, and she wants to estimate what proportion of the songs are by a female artist. She takes an [[☃ definition 1]] of $50$ songs on her phone and finds that $20$ of the songs sampled are by a female artist.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $99\%$ confidence interval for the proportion of songs on her phone that are by a female artist?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a one-sample $z$ interval for a proportion:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard deviation}}{\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\hat p &\pm z^{*} \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p(1- \hat p)}{n}}
\end{align}$

Note that we have met all of the conditions for using this formula:

* The data came from a random sample from the population.
* There were at least $10$ successes and $10$ failures since $20$ songs were by a female artist and the other $30$ weren't.
* We can treat the songs as independent since $n=50$ is less than $10\%$ of the population of more than $500$ songs.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion $\hat p$:

$\hat p=\dfrac{20\text{ songs by females}}{50 \text{ sampled}}=0.40$

Our sample size is $n=50$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $99\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=2.576.$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
Elena wants to estimate what proportion of computers produced at a factory have a certain defect. A random sample of $200$ computers shows that $12$ computers have the defect. She is willing to assume independence between computers in the sample. 

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the proportion of computers that have the defect?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a one-sample $z$ interval for a proportion:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard deviation}}{\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\hat p &\pm z^{*} \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p(1- \hat p)}{n}}
\end{align}$

Note that we have met all of the conditions for using this formula:

* The data came from a random sample from the population.
* There were at least $10$ successes and $10$ failures since $12$ computers had the defect the other $188$ didn't.
* They are assuming independence between computers in the sample, which seems reasonable.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion $\hat p$:

$\hat p=\dfrac{12\text{ defects}}{200 \text{ sampled}}=0.06$

Our sample size is $n=200$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $95\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=1.96.$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Leandro wants to estimate the proportion of light bulbs that arrive broken upon shipment to his store. He takes a random sample of $500$ bulbs and finds that $25$ arrived broken. He's willing to assume independence between bulbs in the sample.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $90\%$ confidence interval for the proportion of bulbs that arrive broken?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a one-sample $z$ interval for a proportion:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard deviation}}{\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\hat p &\pm z^{*} \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p(1- \hat p)}{n}}
\end{align}$

Note that we have met all of the conditions for using this formula:

* The data came from a random sample from the population.
* There were at least $10$ successes and $10$ failures since $25$ bulbs arrived broken and the other $475$ didn't.
* They are assuming independence between bulbs in the sample, which seems reasonable.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion $\hat p$:

$\hat p=\dfrac{25\text{ broken}}{500 \text{ sampled}}=0.05$

Our sample size is $n=500$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $90\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=1.645.$

[[☃ explanation 3]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Yun is curious what proportion of users will click an advertisement that appears on his website. He takes a random sample of $200$ users and finds that $34$ of them clicked the advertisement. He's willing to assume independence between users in the sample.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $99\%$ confidence interval for the proportion of users that click the ad?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a one-sample $z$ interval for a proportion:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard deviation}}{\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\hat p &\pm z^{*} \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p(1- \hat p)}{n}}
\end{align}$

Note that we have met all of the conditions for using this formula:

* The data came from a random sample from the population.
* There were at least $10$ successes and $10$ failures since $34$ users clicked the advertisement and the other $166$ didn't.
* They are assuming independence between users in the sample, which seems reasonable.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion $\hat p$:

$\hat p=\dfrac{34\text{ clicked}}{200 \text{ sampled}}=0.17$

Our sample size is $n=200$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $99\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=2.576.$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Fatimah wants to estimate the proportion of students at her school who ate breakfast every day last week. She takes an [[☃ definition 1]] of $80$ of the $1000$ students at her school and finds that $20$ of those sampled ate breakfast every day last week.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the proportion of all students at the school who ate breakfast every day last week?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a one-sample $z$ interval for a proportion:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard deviation}}{\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\hat p &\pm z^{*} \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p(1- \hat p)}{n}}
\end{align}$

Note that we have met all of the conditions for using this formula:

* The data came from a random sample from the population.
* There were at least $10$ successes and $10$ failures since $20$ ate breakfast and the other $60$ didn't.
* We can treat the students as independent since $n=80$ is less than $10\%$ of the population of $1000$ students.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion $\hat p$:

$\hat p=\dfrac{20\text{ ate breakfast}}{80 \text{ sampled}}=0.25$

Our sample size is $n=80$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $95\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=1.96.$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A political pollster wants to know what proportion of voters are planning to vote for the incumbent candidate in an upcoming election. A poll of $150$ randomly selected voters is taken from the more than $2{,}000$ voters in the population, and $78$ of those selected plan to vote for the incumbent candidate.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $90\%$ confidence interval for the proportion of all voters who plan to vote for the incumbent candidate?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a one-sample $z$ interval for a proportion:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard deviation}}{\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\hat p &\pm z^{*} \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p(1- \hat p)}{n}}
\end{align}$

Note that we have met all of the conditions for using this formula:

* The data came from a random sample from the population.
* There were at least $10$ successes and $10$ failures since $78$ said they plan to vote for the incumbent and the other $72$ didn't.
* We can treat the people in the sample as independent since $n=150$ is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem says there are more $2{,}000$ voters in the population).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion $\hat p$:

$\hat p=\dfrac{78\text{ incumbent voters}}{150 \text{ sampled}}=0.52$

Our sample size is $n=150$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $90\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=1.645.$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A school administrator wants to know what proportion of the approximately $80{,}000$ teachers in their state have a Master's degree. The administrator takes an [[☃ definition 1]] of $100$ teachers from a statewide database containing every teacher, and they find $55$ teachers in the sample have a Master's degree.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $99\%$ confidence interval for the proportion of teachers in the state with a Master's degree?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a one-sample $z$ interval for a proportion:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard deviation}}{\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\hat p &\pm z^{*} \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p(1- \hat p)}{n}}
\end{align}$

Note that we have met all of the conditions for using this formula:

* The data came from a random sample from the population.
* There were at least $10$ successes and $10$ failures since $55$ teachers had a Master's degree and the other $45$ didn't.
* We can treat the teachers in the sample as independent since $n=100$ is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem says there are approximately $80{,}000$ teachers in the population).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion $\hat p$:

$\hat p=\dfrac{55\text{ with Master's}}{100 \text{ sampled}}=0.55$

Our sample size is $n=100$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $99\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=2.576.$

[[☃ explanation 3]]

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
A market analyst is curious what proportion of Los Angeles residents have a landline telephone. A survey of $200$ randomly selected Los Angeles residents shows that $96$ of those selected have a landline telephone. The analyst wants to use this data to construct a one-sample $z$ interval for a proportion.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $90\%$ confidence interval for the proportion of Los Angeles residents who have a landline telephone?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a one-sample $z$ interval for a proportion:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard deviation}}{\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\hat p &\pm z^{*} \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p(1- \hat p)}{n}}
\end{align}$

Note that we have met all of the conditions for using this formula:

* The data came from a random sample from the population.
* There were at least $10$ successes and $10$ failures since $96$ residents had a landline telephone and the other $104$ didn't.
* We can treat the residents in the sample as independent since $n=200$ is less than $10\%$ of the population (there are more than $2{,}000$ residents in Los Angeles).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion $\hat p$:

$\hat p=\dfrac{96\text{ with landline}}{200 \text{ sampled}}=0.48$

Our sample size is $n=200$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $90\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=1.645.$

[[☃ explanation 4]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Gallup is a company that carries out daily opinion polls on a variety of topics. In a recent daily poll of $1{,}000$ randomly selected US adults, $334$ said they were engaged at work.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $90\%$ confidence interval for the proportion of all US adults who would say they are engaged at work?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a one-sample $z$ interval for a proportion:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard deviation}}{\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\hat p &\pm z^{*} \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p(1- \hat p)}{n}}
\end{align}$

Note that we have met all of the conditions for using this formula:

* The data came from a random sample from the population.
* There were at least $10$ successes and $10$ failures since $334$ said they were engaged and the other $666$ didn't.
* We can treat the adults as independent since $n=1000$ is less than $10\%$ of the population of all US adults.

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion $\hat p$:

$\hat p=\dfrac{334\text{ engaged}}{1000 \text{ sampled}}=0.334$

Our sample size is $n=1000$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $90\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=1.645.$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
An airline transports over $10{,}000$ passengers daily, and the airline is curious what proportion of their passengers use mobile boarding passes instead of paper passes. They take an [[☃ definition 1]] of $80$ passengers and find that $60$ of them use mobile boarding passes.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the proportion of passengers who use mobile boarding passes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a one-sample $z$ interval for a proportion:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard deviation}}{\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\hat p &\pm z^{*} \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p(1- \hat p)}{n}}
\end{align}$

Note that we have met all of the conditions for using this formula:

* The data came from a random sample from the population.
* There were at least $10$ successes and $10$ failures since $60$ passengers used a mobile boarding pass and the other $20$ didn't.
* We can treat the passengers as independent since $n=80$ is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem says they transport over $10{,}000$ passengers daily).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion $\hat p$:

$\hat p=\dfrac{60\text{ used mobile}}{80 \text{ sampled}}=0.75$

Our sample size is $n=80$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $95\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=1.96.$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

options.definition: A simple random sample (SRS) is a sample taken so that each member and set of $n$ members has an equal chance of being in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
Amy works for a company that produces plastic lids and cups for restaurants. She wants to estimate what proportion of lids produced in a day have a defect that makes them not fit the cups. She takes a random sample of $250$ lids from the more than $10{,}000$ total lids produced that day and finds $15$ lids in the sample with this defect.

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $99\%$ confidence interval for the proportion of lids produced that day with this defect?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula:

Here's the formula for a one-sample $z$ interval for a proportion:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard deviation}}{\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\hat p &\pm z^{*} \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p(1- \hat p)}{n}}
\end{align}$

Note that we have met all of the conditions for using this formula:

* The data came from a random sample from the population.
* There were at least $10$ successes and $10$ failures since $15$ lids had the defect and the other $235$ didn't.
* We can treat the lids as independent since $n=250$ is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem says they produce more than $10{,}000$ lids that day).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion $\hat p$:

$\hat p=\dfrac{15\text{ had defect}}{250 \text{ sampled}}=0.06$

Our sample size is $n=250$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $99\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=2.576.$

[[☃ explanation 3]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A survey of $400$ randomly selected homes from a large city with over $30{,}000$ homes showed that $16$ of the sampled homes didn't have a television. 

**Based on this sample, which of the following is a $95\%$ confidence interval for the proportion of homes in this city that don't have a television?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formula

Here's the formula for a one-sample $z$ interval for a proportion:

$\begin{align}
(\text{statistic}) &\pm (\text{critical value})\left(\overset{\text{standard deviation}}{\text{of statistic}}\right) \\\\
\hat p &\pm z^{*} \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat p(1- \hat p)}{n}}
\end{align}$

Note that we have met all of the conditions for using this formula:

* The data came from a random sample from the population.
* There were at least $10$ successes and $10$ failures since $16$ homes didn't have a television and the other $384$ did.
* We can treat the homes as independent since $n=400$ is less than $10\%$ of the population (the problem says there are more than $30{,}000$ homes in the city).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample proportion $\hat p$:

$\hat p=\dfrac{16\text{ no TV}}{400 \text{ sampled}}=0.04$

Our sample size is $n=400$.

Our critical value $z^{*}$ for $95\%$ confidence is $z^{*}=1.96.$

[[☃ explanation 4]]
